title: The Humanity of Christ
intro: He wept. Two words in John 11:35 that carry the weight of the entire incarnation. The God who created all things stood at a tomb and wept. Not because He did not know what He was about to do — but because grief is real, and He was fully human.
reading:
Philippians 2:6-8 describes the kenosis — the self-emptying. Jesus, though He was in the form of God, did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied Himself, taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men. He did not stop being God. He added humanity — with all its vulnerability, limitation, and suffering.

Luke 2:7 places Him in a manger. John 4:6 finds Him wearied from a journey, sitting by a well. He grew hungry in the wilderness. He slept in boats during storms. He had friends He loved and grieved when they suffered. John 11:35 records the shortest and perhaps most profound verse in all of Scripture: Jesus wept.

Hebrews 2:14-18 tells us why this matters so deeply: because He Himself has suffered when tempted, He is able to help those who are being tempted. His humanity is not incidental — it is the very thing that makes Him the merciful and faithful high priest.

Hebrews 4:15 gives the great comfort of the incarnation: we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin.

He is not a distant God observing your struggle from outside. He entered it. He knows what it is to be tired, lonely, misunderstood, and afraid.
reflection: Is there a struggle, temptation, or form of suffering in your life that you have felt too ashamed or too human to bring to Jesus? What does His full humanity give you permission to do with that?
abide: Jesus, You know what it is to be human — truly, fully, without pretense. I bring to You what I have been carrying alone. You are not scandalized by my weakness. You bore it Yourself.
